The **Central African Republic**[[a]](#cite_note-12) (**CAR**) is a [landlocked country](/wiki/Landlocked_country "Landlocked country") located in [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa"). It is bordered by [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad") to [the north](/wiki/Central_African_Republic%E2%80%93Chad_border "Central African Republic–Chad border"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") to [the northeast](/wiki/Central_African_Republic%E2%80%93Sudan_border "Central African Republic–Sudan border"), [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan") to [the east](/wiki/Central_African_Republic%E2%80%93South_Sudan_border "Central African Republic–South Sudan border"), the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") to [the south](/wiki/Central_African_Republic%E2%80%93Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo_border "Central African Republic–Democratic Republic of the Congo border"), the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo") to [the southwest](/wiki/Central_African_Republic%E2%80%93Republic_of_the_Congo_border "Central African Republic–Republic of the Congo border"), and [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") to [the west](/wiki/Cameroon%E2%80%93Central_African_Republic_border "Cameroon–Central African Republic border"). The Central African Republic covers a land area of about 620,000 square kilometres (240,000 sq mi). As of 2024, it has a population of 5,357,744, consisting of about 80 ethnic groups.[[12]](#cite_note-13) Having been a French colony under the name [Ubangi-Shari](/wiki/Ubangi-Shari "Ubangi-Shari"),[[b]](#cite_note-14) [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") is its [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"), with [Sango](/wiki/Sango_language "Sango language"), a [Ngbandi](/wiki/Ngbandi_language "Ngbandi language")-based [creole language](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language"), as the national and co-official language.[[1]](#cite_note-Samarin_2000_301–334-1)[[13]](#cite_note-profile-15) Its capital and largest city is [Bangui](/wiki/Bangui "Bangui"), which is on the southern border with the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo").

The Central African Republic mainly consists of Sudano-Guinean [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna"), but the country also includes a [Sahelo](/wiki/Sahel "Sahel")-[Sudanian](/wiki/East_Sudanian_savanna "East Sudanian savanna") zone in the north and an [equatorial forest zone](/wiki/Northern_Congolian_forest-savanna_mosaic "Northern Congolian forest-savanna mosaic") in the south. Two-thirds of the country is within the basin of the [Ubangi River](/wiki/Ubangi_River "Ubangi River") (which flows into the [Congo River](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River")), while the remaining third lies in the basin of the [Chari River](/wiki/Chari_River "Chari River"), which flows into [Lake Chad](/wiki/Lake_Chad "Lake Chad").

What is today the Central African Republic has been inhabited since at least 8000 BCE. The country's borders were established by France, which began annexing portions to the [French Congo](/wiki/French_Congo "French Congo") in the late 19th century and in 1903 established the separate colony of [Ubangi-Shari](/wiki/Ubangi-Shari "Ubangi-Shari"), part of [French Equatorial Africa](/wiki/French_Equatorial_Africa "French Equatorial Africa"). After gaining independence from France in 1960, the Central African Republic was ruled by a series of autocratic leaders, including [Jean-Bedel Bokassa](/wiki/Jean-Bedel_Bokassa "Jean-Bedel Bokassa") who changed the country's name to the [Central African Empire](/wiki/Central_African_Empire "Central African Empire") and ruled as a monarch from 1976 to 1979.[[14]](#cite_note-Bokassa-16) The [Central African Republic Bush War](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Bush_War "Central African Republic Bush War") began in 2004 and, despite a peace treaty in 2007 and another in 2011, [civil war resumed](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Civil_War "Central African Republic Civil War") in 2012. The civil war perpetuated the country's poor [human rights record](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Human rights in the Central African Republic"), characterized by widespread and increasing abuses by various participating armed groups.

Despite the country's vast amounts of [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources"), such as [uranium](/wiki/Uranium "Uranium") reserves, [crude oil](/wiki/Crude_oil "Crude oil"), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [diamonds](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond"), [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), [lumber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber"), and [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"),[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17) as well as significant quantities of [arable land](/wiki/Arable_land "Arable land"), the Central African Republic is among the ten poorest countries in the world, with the lowest [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") per capita at purchasing power parity (PPP) in the world as of 2017.[[16]](#cite_note-IMF_2017-18) As of 2023, according to the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI), the country had the third-lowest level of human development, ranking 191 out of 193 countries. The country had the second-lowest [inequality-adjusted Human Development Index](/wiki/Inequality-adjusted_Human_Development_Index "Inequality-adjusted Human Development Index") (IHDI), ranking 164th out of 165 countries.[[17]](#cite_note-HDI-19) The Central African Republic is also estimated to be the unhealthiest country,[[18]](#cite_note-20) as well as the worst country to be in for young people.[[19]](#cite_note-21) The Central African Republic is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union"), the [Economic Community of Central African States](/wiki/Economic_Community_of_Central_African_States "Economic Community of Central African States"), the [Organisation internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie") and the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The name of the Central African Republic is derived from the country's geographical location in the central region of Africa and its republican form of government. From 1976 to 1979, the country was known as the [Central African Empire](/wiki/Central_African_Empire "Central African Empire").

During the colonial era, the country's name was [Ubangi-Shari](/wiki/Ubangi-Shari "Ubangi-Shari") ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Oubangui-Chari*), a name derived from two major rivers and Central African [waterways](/wiki/Waterway "Waterway") – the [Ubangi](/wiki/Ubangi_River "Ubangi River") and the [Chari](/wiki/Chari_River "Chari River"). [Barthélemy Boganda](/wiki/Barth%C3%A9lemy_Boganda "Barthélemy Boganda"), the country's first [prime minister](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_government_of_the_Central_African_Republic "List of heads of government of the Central African Republic"), favored the name "Central African Republic" over Ubangi-Shari, reportedly because he envisioned [a larger union of countries](/wiki/United_States_of_Latin_Africa "United States of Latin Africa") in [Central Africa](/wiki/Central_Africa "Central Africa").[[20]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Central African Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Central_African_Republic "History of the Central African Republic")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/8/80/Bouar_Megaliths_stamp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bouar_Megaliths_stamp.jpg)

The [Bouar](/wiki/Bouar "Bouar") Megaliths, pictured here on a 1967 Central African stamp, date back to the very late Neolithic Era (c. 3500–2700 BCE).

Approximately 10,000 years ago, [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification") forced [hunter-gatherer](/wiki/Hunter-gatherer "Hunter-gatherer") societies south into the Sahel regions of northern Central Africa, where some groups settled.[[21]](#cite_note-Eastern_Africa_2011,_pg._4-23) Farming began as part of the [Neolithic Revolution](/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution "Neolithic Revolution").[[22]](#cite_note-24) Initial farming of [white yam](/wiki/Dioscorea_rotundata "Dioscorea rotundata") progressed into [millet](/wiki/Millet "Millet") and [sorghum](/wiki/Sorghum "Sorghum"), and before 3000 BCE[[23]](#cite_note-25) the domestication of [African oil palm](/wiki/Elaeis_guineensis "Elaeis guineensis") improved the groups' nutrition and allowed for expansion of the local populations.[[24]](#cite_note-26) This agricultural revolution, combined with a "Fish-stew Revolution", in which fishing began to take place and the use of boats, allowed for the transportation of goods. Products were often moved in [ceramic](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") pots.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Bouar Megaliths](/wiki/Bouar "Bouar") in the western region of the country indicate an advanced level of habitation dating back to the very late [Neolithic Era](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") (c. 3500–2700 BCE).[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) [Ironwork](/wiki/Ferrous_metallurgy "Ferrous metallurgy") developed in the region around 1000 BCE.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

The [Ubangian people](/wiki/Ubangian_languages "Ubangian languages") settled along the [Ubangi River](/wiki/Ubangi_River "Ubangi River") in what is today the Central and East Central African Republic while some [Bantu people](/wiki/Bantu_peoples "Bantu peoples") migrated from the southwest of [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon").[[28]](#cite_note-30)

[Bananas](/wiki/Banana "Banana") arrived in the region during the first millennium BCE[[29]](#cite_note-31) and added an important source of carbohydrates to the diet; they were also used in the production of [alcoholic beverages](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverages "Alcoholic beverages"). Production of [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt"), [dried fish](/wiki/Dried_fish "Dried fish"), and [textiles](/wiki/Textiles "Textiles") dominated the economic trade in the Central African region.[[30]](#cite_note-32)

### 16th–19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 16th–19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Sultan_and_his_wives_at_Bangassou%2C_1906.png/250px-Sultan_and_his_wives_at_Bangassou%2C_1906.png)](/wiki/File:Sultan_and_his_wives_at_Bangassou,_1906.png)

The Sultan of [Bangassou](/wiki/Bangassou "Bangassou") and his wives, 1906

In the 16th and 17th centuries, [slave traders](/wiki/History_of_slavery "History of slavery") began to raid the region as part of the expansion of the Saharan and Nile River slave routes. Their captives were enslaved and shipped to the Mediterranean coast, Europe, Arabia, the Western Hemisphere, or to the slave ports and factories along the West and North Africa or South along the Ubangui and Congo rivers.[[31]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-africanhistory.about.com-34) During the 18th century Bandia-Nzakara [Azande](/wiki/Azande "Azande") peoples established the [Bangassou](/wiki/Bangassou "Bangassou") Kingdom along the [Ubangi River](/wiki/Ubangi_River "Ubangi River").[[32]](#cite_note-africanhistory.about.com-34) In the mid 19th century, the [Bobangi people](/wiki/Bangi_language "Bangi language") became major slave traders and sold their captives to the Americas using the Ubangi river to reach the coast.[[33]](#cite_note-35) In 1875, the [Sudanese](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") sultan [Rabih az-Zubayr](/wiki/Rabih_az-Zubayr "Rabih az-Zubayr") governed Upper-Oubangui, which included present-day Central African Republic.[[34]](#cite_note-Zubayr-36)

### French colonial period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: French colonial period")]

Main articles: [Ubangi-Shari](/wiki/Ubangi-Shari "Ubangi-Shari"), [French Equatorial Africa](/wiki/French_Equatorial_Africa "French Equatorial Africa"), and [Colonialism in the Central African Republic](/wiki/Colonialism_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Colonialism in the Central African Republic")

The European invasion of Central African territory began in the late 19th century during the [Scramble for Africa](/wiki/Scramble_for_Africa "Scramble for Africa").[[35]](#cite_note-37) Europeans, primarily the French, [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), and [Belgians](/wiki/Belgians "Belgians"), arrived in the area in 1885. France seized and colonized [Ubangi-Shari](/wiki/Ubangi-Shari "Ubangi-Shari") territory in 1894. In 1911 at the [Treaty of Fez](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fez "Treaty of Fez"), France ceded a nearly 300,000 km2 portion of the Sangha and Lobaye basins to the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") which ceded a smaller area (in present-day [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad")) to France. After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") France again annexed the territory. Modeled on [King Leopold](/wiki/Leopold_II_of_Belgium "Leopold II of Belgium")'s [Congo Free State](/wiki/Congo_Free_State "Congo Free State"), concessions were doled out to private companies that endeavored to strip the region's assets as quickly and cheaply as possible before depositing a percentage of their profits into the French treasury. The concessionary companies [forced local people](/wiki/Corv%C3%A9e "Corvée") to harvest rubber, coffee, and other commodities without pay and held their families hostage until they met their quotas.[[36]](#cite_note-fp1015-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/De_Gaulle_Bangui_1940.jpg/250px-De_Gaulle_Bangui_1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_Gaulle_Bangui_1940.jpg)

[Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") in Bangui, 1940

In 1920, [French Equatorial Africa](/wiki/French_Equatorial_Africa "French Equatorial Africa") was established and Ubangi-Shari was administered from [Brazzaville](/wiki/Brazzaville "Brazzaville").[[37]](#cite_note-Francophone_Africa_1997,_pg._111-39) During the 1920s and 1930s the French introduced a policy of mandatory cotton cultivation,[[37]](#cite_note-Francophone_Africa_1997,_pg._111-39) a network of roads were built, attempts were made to combat [sleeping sickness](/wiki/African_trypanosomiasis "African trypanosomiasis"), and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") [missions](/wiki/Mission_(Christianity) "Mission (Christianity)") were established to spread Christianity.[[38]](#cite_note-40) New forms of forced labour were also introduced and a large number of Ubangians were sent to work on the [Congo-Ocean Railway](/wiki/Congo-Ocean_Railway "Congo-Ocean Railway"). Through the period of construction until 1934 there was a continual heavy cost in human lives, with total deaths among all workers along the railway estimated in excess of 17,000 of the construction workers, from a combination of both industrial accidents and diseases including [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria").[[39]](#cite_note-41) In 1928, a major insurrection, the [Kongo-Wara rebellion](/wiki/Kongo-Wara_rebellion "Kongo-Wara rebellion") or 'war of the hoe handle', broke out in Western Ubangi-Shari and continued for several years. The extent of this insurrection, which was perhaps the largest anti-colonial rebellion in Africa during the interwar years, was carefully hidden from the French public because it provided evidence of strong opposition to French colonial rule and forced labour.[[40]](#cite_note-42)
French colonization in Oubangui-Chari is considered to be the most brutal of the French colonial Empire.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

In September 1940, during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), [pro-Gaullist](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle "Charles de Gaulle") French officers took control of Ubangi-Shari and [General Leclerc](/wiki/General_Leclerc "General Leclerc") established his headquarters for the [Free French Forces](/wiki/Free_French_Forces "Free French Forces") in [Bangui](/wiki/Bangui "Bangui").[[42]](#cite_note-44) In 1946 [Barthélemy Boganda](/wiki/Barth%C3%A9lemy_Boganda "Barthélemy Boganda") was elected with 9,000 votes to the [French National Assembly](/wiki/French_National_Assembly "French National Assembly"), becoming the first representative of the Central African Republic in the French government. Boganda maintained a political stance against racism and the colonial regime but gradually became disheartened with the French political system and returned to the Central African Republic to establish the [Movement for the Social Evolution of Black Africa](/wiki/Movement_for_the_Social_Evolution_of_Black_Africa "Movement for the Social Evolution of Black Africa") (*Mouvement pour l'évolution sociale de l'Afrique noire*, MESAN) in 1950.[[34]](#cite_note-Zubayr-36)

### Since independence (1960–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Since independence (1960–present)")]

In the [Ubangi-Shari Territorial Assembly election](/wiki/1957_Ubangi-Shari_parliamentary_election "1957 Ubangi-Shari parliamentary election") in 1957, MESAN captured 347,000 out of the total 356,000 votes[[43]](#cite_note-45) and won every legislative seat,[[44]](#cite_note-46) which led to Boganda being elected president of the Grand Council of [French Equatorial Africa](/wiki/French_Equatorial_Africa "French Equatorial Africa") and vice-president of the Ubangi-Shari Government Council.[[45]](#cite_note-47) Within a year, he declared the establishment of the Central African Republic and served as the country's first prime minister. MESAN continued to exist, but its role was limited.[[46]](#cite_note-Kalck2005p136-48) The Central African Republic was granted autonomy within the French Community on 1 December 1958, a status which meant it was still counted as part of the French Empire in Africa.[[47]](#cite_note-49)

After Boganda's death in a [plane crash](/wiki/Barth%C3%A9lemy_Boganda#Plane_crash "Barthélemy Boganda") on 29 March 1959, his cousin, [David Dacko](/wiki/David_Dacko "David Dacko"), took control of MESAN. Dacko became the country's first president when the Central African Republic formally received [independence](/wiki/Independence "Independence") from France at midnight on 13 August 1960, a date celebrated by the country's [Independence Day](/wiki/List_of_national_independence_days "List of national independence days") holiday.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Dacko threw out his political rivals, including [Abel Goumba](/wiki/Abel_Goumba "Abel Goumba"), former Prime Minister and leader of [Mouvement d'évolution démocratique de l'Afrique centrale](/wiki/Democratic_Evolution_Movement_of_Central_Africa "Democratic Evolution Movement of Central Africa") (MEDAC), whom he forced into exile in France. With all opposition parties suppressed by November 1962, Dacko declared MESAN as the official party of the state.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

#### Bokassa and the Central African Empire (1965–1979)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Bokassa and the Central African Empire (1965–1979)")]

Further information: [Central African Empire](/wiki/Central_African_Empire "Central African Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Bokassa_with_Ceausescu_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bokassa_with_Ceausescu_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bokassa_with_Ceausescu_(cropped).jpg)

[Jean-Bédel Bokassa](/wiki/Jean-B%C3%A9del_Bokassa "Jean-Bédel Bokassa"), self-crowned [Emperor of Central Africa](/wiki/Emperor_of_Central_Africa "Emperor of Central Africa")[[14]](#cite_note-Bokassa-16)

On 31 December 1965, Dacko was overthrown in the [Saint-Sylvestre coup d'état](/wiki/Saint-Sylvestre_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Saint-Sylvestre coup d'état") by Colonel [Jean-Bédel Bokassa](/wiki/Jean-B%C3%A9del_Bokassa "Jean-Bédel Bokassa"), who suspended the constitution and dissolved the National Assembly. President Bokassa declared himself President for Life in 1972 and named himself Emperor Bokassa I of the [Central African Empire](/wiki/Central_African_Empire "Central African Empire") (as the country was renamed) on 4 December 1976. A year later, Emperor Bokassa [crowned himself in an expensive ceremony](/wiki/Coronation_of_Bokassa_I_and_Catherine "Coronation of Bokassa I and Catherine").[[14]](#cite_note-Bokassa-16)

In April 1979, young students protested against Bokassa's decree that all school pupils were required to buy uniforms from a company owned by one of his wives. The government violently suppressed the protests, killing 100 children and teenagers. Bokassa might have been personally involved in some of the killings.[[50]](#cite_note-52) In September 1979, [France overthrew Bokassa](/wiki/Operation_Caban "Operation Caban") and restored Dacko to power (subsequently restoring the official name of the country and the original government to the Central African Republic). Dacko, in turn, was again overthrown in a [coup](/wiki/1981_Central_African_Republic_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1981 Central African Republic coup d'état") by General [André Kolingba](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Kolingba "André Kolingba") on 1 September 1981.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

#### Central African Republic under Kolingba

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Central African Republic under Kolingba")]

Kolingba suspended the constitution and ruled with a [military junta](/wiki/Military_junta "Military junta") until 1985. He introduced a new constitution in 1986 which was adopted by a nationwide referendum. Membership in his new party, the [Rassemblement Démocratique Centrafricain](/wiki/Rassemblement_D%C3%A9mocratique_Centrafricain "Rassemblement Démocratique Centrafricain") (RDC), was voluntary. In 1987 and 1988, semi-free elections to parliament were held, but Kolingba's two major political opponents, [Abel Goumba](/wiki/Abel_Goumba "Abel Goumba") and [Ange-Félix Patassé](/wiki/Ange-F%C3%A9lix_Patass%C3%A9 "Ange-Félix Patassé"), were not allowed to participate.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-54)

By 1990, inspired by the [fall of the Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall"), a pro-democracy movement arose. Pressure from the United States, France, and from a group of locally represented countries and agencies called GIBAFOR (France, the US, Germany, Japan, the EU, the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank"), and the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations")) finally led Kolingba to agree, in principle, to hold free elections in October 1992 with help from the UN Office of Electoral Affairs. After using the excuse of alleged irregularities to suspend the results of the elections as a pretext for holding on to power, President Kolingba came under intense pressure from GIBAFOR to establish a "Conseil National Politique Provisoire de la République" (Provisional National Political Council, CNPPR) and to set up a "Mixed Electoral Commission", which included representatives from all political parties.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-54)

When a second round of elections were finally held in 1993, again with the help of the international community coordinated by GIBAFOR, Ange-Félix Patassé won in the second round of voting with 53% of the vote while Goumba won 45.6%. Patassé's party, the *Mouvement pour la Libération du Peuple Centrafricain* (MLPC) or Movement for the Liberation of the Central African People, gained a [plurality](/wiki/Plurality_voting "Plurality voting") (relative majority) but not an [absolute majority](/wiki/Majority "Majority") of seats in parliament, which meant Patassé's party required coalition partners.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-54)

#### Patassé government (1993–2003)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Patassé government (1993–2003)")]

Patassé purged many of the Kolingba elements from the government and Kolingba supporters accused Patassé's government of conducting a "[witch hunt](/wiki/Witch-hunt#Figurative_use_of_the_term "Witch-hunt")" against the Yakoma. A new constitution was approved on 28 December 1994 but had little impact on the country's politics. In 1996–1997, reflecting steadily decreasing public confidence in the government's erratic behavior, three [mutinies](/wiki/Mutiny "Mutiny") against Patassé's administration were accompanied by widespread destruction of property and heightened ethnic tension. During this time (1996), the [Peace Corps](/wiki/Peace_Corps "Peace Corps") evacuated all its volunteers to neighboring Cameroon. To date, the Peace Corps has not returned to the Central African Republic. The [Bangui Agreements](/wiki/Bangui_Agreements "Bangui Agreements"), signed in January 1997, provided for the deployment of an inter-African military mission, to the Central African Republic and re-entry of ex-mutineers into the government on 7 April 1997. The inter-African military mission was later replaced by a U.N. [peacekeeping force (MINURCA)](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_1159 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 1159"). Since 1997, the country has hosted almost a dozen peacekeeping interventions, earning it the title of "world champion of peacekeeping".[[36]](#cite_note-fp1015-38)

In 1998, parliamentary elections resulted in Kolingba's RDC winning 20 out of 109 seats. The next year, however, in spite of widespread public anger in urban centers over his corrupt rule, Patassé won a second term in the presidential election.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

On 28 May 2001, rebels stormed strategic buildings in Bangui in an [unsuccessful coup attempt](/wiki/2001_Central_African_Republic_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "2001 Central African Republic coup d'état attempt"). The army chief of staff, Abel Abrou, and General François N'Djadder Bedaya were killed, but Patassé regained the upper hand by bringing in at least 300 troops of the [Congolese](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") rebel leader [Jean-Pierre Bemba](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Bemba "Jean-Pierre Bemba") and Libyan soldiers.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

In the aftermath of the [failed coup](/wiki/2001_Central_African_Republic_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "2001 Central African Republic coup d'état attempt"), militias loyal to Patassé sought revenge against rebels in many neighborhoods of [Bangui](/wiki/Bangui "Bangui") and incited unrest including the murder of many political opponents. Eventually, Patassé came to suspect that General [François Bozizé](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boziz%C3%A9 "François Bozizé") was involved in another coup attempt against him, which led Bozizé to flee with loyal troops to Chad. In March 2003, Bozizé launched a surprise attack against Patassé, who was out of the country. Libyan troops and some 1,000 soldiers of Bemba's Congolese rebel organization failed to stop the rebels and Bozizé's forces succeeded in overthrowing Patassé.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

#### Civil wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Civil wars")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Rebel_in_northern_CAR_01.jpg/250px-Rebel_in_northern_CAR_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rebel_in_northern_CAR_01.jpg)

Rebel militia in the northern countryside, 2007

See also: [Central African Republic Bush War](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Bush_War "Central African Republic Bush War") and [Central African Republic Civil War](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Civil_War "Central African Republic Civil War")

[François Bozizé](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boziz%C3%A9 "François Bozizé") suspended the constitution and named a new cabinet, which included most opposition parties. [Abel Goumba](/wiki/Abel_Goumba "Abel Goumba") was named vice-president. Bozizé established a broad-based National Transition Council to draft a new constitution, and announced that he would step down and run for office once the new constitution was approved.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

In 2004, the [Central African Republic Bush War](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Bush_War "Central African Republic Bush War") began as forces opposed to Bozizé took up arms against his government. In May 2005, Bozizé won the presidential election, which excluded Patassé, and in 2006 fighting continued between the government and the rebels.[[57]](#cite_note-59) In November 2006, Bozizé's government requested French military support to help them repel rebels who had taken control of towns in the country's northern regions.[[58]](#cite_note-bbc20061114-60) Though the initial public details of the agreement pertained to logistics and intelligence, by December the French assistance included airstrikes by [Dassault Mirage 2000](/wiki/Dassault_Mirage_2000 "Dassault Mirage 2000") fighters against rebel positions.[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-bbc20061130-62)

The Syrte Agreement in February and the Birao Peace Agreement in April 2007 called for a cessation of hostilities, the billeting of [FDPC](/wiki/Democratic_Front_of_the_Central_African_People "Democratic Front of the Central African People") fighters and their integration with FACA, the liberation of political prisoners, the integration of FDPC into government, an amnesty for the [UFDR](/wiki/Union_of_Democratic_Forces_for_Unity "Union of Democratic Forces for Unity"), its recognition as a political party, and the integration of its fighters into the national army. Several groups continued to fight but other groups signed on to the agreement or similar agreements with the government (e.g., UFR on 15 December 2008). The only major group not to sign an agreement at the time was the [CPJP](/wiki/Convention_of_Patriots_for_Justice_and_Peace "Convention of Patriots for Justice and Peace"), which continued its activities and signed a peace agreement with the government on 25 August 2012.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

In 2011, Bozizé was reelected in an election which was widely considered fraudulent.[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17)

In November 2012, [Séléka](/wiki/S%C3%A9l%C3%A9ka "Séléka"), a coalition of rebel groups, took over towns in the northern and central regions of the country. These groups eventually reached a peace deal with Bozizé's government in January 2013, involving a power-sharing government.[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17) The deal later broke down, and the rebels seized the capital in March 2013 and Bozizé fled the country.[[62]](#cite_note-cnn20130324-64)[[63]](#cite_note-nyt20130325-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Refugees_of_the_fighting_in_the_Central_African_Republic_observe_Rwandan_soldiers_being_dropped_off_at_Bangui_M%27Poko_International_Airport_in_the_Central_African_Republic_Jan._19%2C_2014_140119-F-RN211-760.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Refugees_of_the_fighting_in_the_Central_African_Republic_observe_Rwandan_soldiers_being_dropped_off_at_Bangui_M%27Poko_International_Airport_in_the_Central_African_Republic_Jan._19,_2014_140119-F-RN211-760.jpg)

[Refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") of the fighting in the Central African Republic, January 2014

[Michel Djotodia](/wiki/Michel_Djotodia "Michel Djotodia") took over as president. Prime Minister [Nicolas Tiangaye](/wiki/Nicolas_Tiangaye "Nicolas Tiangaye") requested a [UN](/wiki/UN "UN") peacekeeping force from the [UN Security Council](/wiki/UN_Security_Council "UN Security Council") and on 31 May former President Bozizé was indicted for crimes against humanity and incitement to genocide.[[64]](#cite_note-66) By the end of the year, there were international warnings of a "genocide"[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-68) and fighting was largely reprisal attacks on civilians by Seleka's predominantly Muslim fighters and Christian militias called "[anti-balaka](/wiki/Anti-balaka "Anti-balaka")".[[67]](#cite_note-GuardianNov13-69) By August 2013, there were reports of over 200,000 [internally displaced persons](/wiki/Internally_displaced_persons "Internally displaced persons") (IDPs).[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71)

On 18 February 2014, [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") Secretary-General [Ban Ki-moon](/wiki/Ban_Ki-moon "Ban Ki-moon") called on the [UN Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") to immediately deploy 3,000 troops to the country, bolstering the 6,000 African Union soldiers and 2,000 French troops already in the country, to combat civilians being murdered in large numbers. The *Séléka* government was said to be divided,[[70]](#cite_note-72) and in September 2013, Djotodia officially disbanded Seleka, but many rebels refused to disarm, becoming known as ex-Seleka, and veered further out of government control.[[67]](#cite_note-GuardianNov13-69) It is argued that the focus of the initial disarmament efforts exclusively on the Seleka inadvertently handed the anti-Balaka the upper hand, leading to the forced displacement of Muslim civilians by anti-Balaka in Bangui and western Central African Republic.[[36]](#cite_note-fp1015-38)

On 11 January 2014, Michael Djotodia and Nicolas Tiengaye resigned as part of a deal negotiated at a regional summit in neighboring [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad").[[71]](#cite_note-73) [Catherine Samba-Panza](/wiki/Catherine_Samba-Panza "Catherine Samba-Panza") was elected interim president by the National Transitional Council,[[72]](#cite_note-74) becoming the first ever female Central African president. On 23 July 2014, following Congolese mediation efforts, Séléka and anti-balaka representatives signed a ceasefire agreement in [Brazzaville](/wiki/Brazzaville "Brazzaville").[[73]](#cite_note-75) By the end of 2014, the country was de facto partitioned with the anti-Balaka in the southwest and ex-Seleka in the northeast.[[36]](#cite_note-fp1015-38) In March 2015, [Samantha Power](/wiki/Samantha_Power "Samantha Power"), the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations, said 417 of the country's 436 mosques had been destroyed, and Muslim women were so scared of going out in public they were giving birth in their homes instead of going to the hospital.[[74]](#cite_note-76) On 14 December 2015, Séléka rebel leaders declared an independent [Republic of Logone](/wiki/Republic_of_Logone "Republic of Logone").[[75]](#cite_note-77)

#### Touadéra government (2016–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Touadéra government (2016–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/War_in_Central_African_Republic.svg/250px-War_in_Central_African_Republic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:War_in_Central_African_Republic.svg)

Current military situation in Central African Republic

[Presidential elections](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Central_African_general_election "2015–16 Central African general election") were held in December 2015. As no candidate received more than 50% of the vote, a second round of elections was held on 14 February 2016 with run-offs on 31 March 2016.[[76]](#cite_note-78)[[77]](#cite_note-79) In the second round of voting, former Prime Minister [Faustin-Archange Touadéra](/wiki/Faustin-Archange_Touad%C3%A9ra "Faustin-Archange Touadéra") was declared the winner with 63% of the vote, defeating [Union for Central African Renewal](/wiki/Union_for_Central_African_Renewal "Union for Central African Renewal") candidate [Anicet-Georges Dologuélé](/wiki/Anicet-Georges_Dologu%C3%A9l%C3%A9 "Anicet-Georges Dologuélé"), another former Prime Minister.[[78]](#cite_note-80) While the elections suffered from many potential voters being absent as they had taken refuge in other countries, the fears of widespread violence were ultimately unfounded, and the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union") regarded the elections as successful.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

Touadéra was sworn in on 30 March 2016. No representatives of the Seleka rebel group or the "anti-balaka" militias were included in the subsequently formed government.[[80]](#cite_note-82)

[Presidential elections](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Central_African_general_election "2020–21 Central African general election") were held on 27 December 2020.[[81]](#cite_note-83) Former president [François Bozizé](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Boziz%C3%A9 "François Bozizé") announced his candidacy but was rejected by the Constitutional Court of the country, which held that Bozizé did not satisfy the "good morality" requirement for candidates because of an international warrant and [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") sanctions against him for alleged assassinations, torture and other crimes.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

As large parts of the country were at the time controlled by armed groups, the election could not be conducted in many areas of the country.[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86) Some 800 of the country's polling stations, or 14% of the total, were closed due to violence.[[85]](#cite_note-87) Three [Burundian](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi") peacekeepers were killed and an additional two were wounded during the run-up to the election.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-UNNews_261220-89) President Faustin-Archange Touadéra was reelected.[[88]](#cite_note-90) Russian mercenaries from the [Wagner Group](/wiki/Wagner_Group "Wagner Group") have supported President Touadéra in the fight against rebels. Russia's Wagner group has been accused of harassing and intimidating civilians.[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-92) In December 2022, [Roger Cohen](/wiki/Roger_Cohen "Roger Cohen") wrote in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* that "[Wagner](/wiki/Wagner_Group "Wagner Group") shock troops form a Praetorian Guard for Mr. Touadéra, who is also protected by Rwandan forces, in return for an untaxed license to exploit and export the Central African Republic's resources" and "one Western ambassador called the Central African Republic...a 'vassal state' of the Kremlin."[[91]](#cite_note-93)
Although the constitution initially limited presidency to two terms, Touadéra abolished constitutional term limits through a controversial referendum in 2023. He was reelected for a third term in December 2025.[[92]](#cite_note-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Geography of the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Republique_Centrafricaine_-_Boali_-_Chutes_de_Boali.jpg/250px-Republique_Centrafricaine_-_Boali_-_Chutes_de_Boali.jpg)](/wiki/File:Republique_Centrafricaine_-_Boali_-_Chutes_de_Boali.jpg)

Falls of Boali on the Mbali River

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/CARvillagefromthesky.jpg/250px-CARvillagefromthesky.jpg)](/wiki/File:CARvillagefromthesky.jpg)

A village in the Central African Republic

The Central African Republic is a landlocked nation within the interior of the African continent. It is bordered by Cameroon, Chad, Sudan, South Sudan, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and the Republic of the Congo. The country lies between latitudes [2°](/wiki/2nd_parallel_north "2nd parallel north") and [11°N](/wiki/11th_parallel_north "11th parallel north"), and longitudes [14°](/wiki/14th_meridian_east "14th meridian east") and [28°E](/wiki/28th_meridian_east "28th meridian east").[[94]](#cite_note-:1-96)

Much of the country consists of flat or rolling plateau [savanna](/wiki/Savanna "Savanna") approximately 500 metres (1,640 ft) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"). In addition to the Fertit Hills in the northeast of the Central African Republic, there are scattered hills in the southwest regions. In the northwest is the Yade Massif, a [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") plateau with an altitude of 348 metres (1,143 ft). The Central African Republic contains six terrestrial ecoregions: [Northeastern Congolian lowland forests](/wiki/Northeastern_Congolian_lowland_forests "Northeastern Congolian lowland forests"), [Northwestern Congolian lowland forests](/wiki/Northwestern_Congolian_lowland_forests "Northwestern Congolian lowland forests"), [Western Congolian swamp forests](/wiki/Western_Congolian_swamp_forests "Western Congolian swamp forests"), [East Sudanian savanna](/wiki/East_Sudanian_savanna "East Sudanian savanna"), [Northern Congolian forest-savanna mosaic](/wiki/Northern_Congolian_forest-savanna_mosaic "Northern Congolian forest-savanna mosaic"), and [Sahelian Acacia savanna](/wiki/Sahelian_Acacia_savanna "Sahelian Acacia savanna").[[95]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-97)

At 622,984 square kilometres (240,535 sq mi), the Central African Republic is the [world's 44th-largest country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area").[[96]](#cite_note-98)

Much of the southern border is formed by [tributaries](/wiki/Tributaries "Tributaries") of the [Congo River](/wiki/Congo_River "Congo River"); the [Mbomou River](/wiki/Mbomou_River "Mbomou River") in the east merges with the [Uele River](/wiki/Uele_River "Uele River") to form the [Ubangi River](/wiki/Ubangi_River "Ubangi River"), which also comprises portions of the southern border. The [Sangha River](/wiki/Sangha_River "Sangha River") flows through some of the western regions of the country, while the eastern border lies along the edge of the [Nile River](/wiki/Nile_River "Nile River") [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin").[[94]](#cite_note-:1-96)

Around 36% of the country is covered by forest, with the densest parts generally located in the southern regions. The forests are highly diverse and include commercially important species of [Ayous](/wiki/Ayous "Ayous"), [Sapelli](/wiki/Sapele "Sapele"), and [Sipo](/wiki/Sipo_(timber) "Sipo (timber)").[[97]](#cite_note-99) The deforestation rate is about 0.4% per annum, and [lumber poaching](/wiki/Illegal_logging "Illegal logging") is commonplace.[[98]](#cite_note-100) The Central African Republic had a 2018 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 9.28/10, ranking it seventh globally out of 172 countries.[[99]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-101)

In 2008, Central African Republic was the world's least [light pollution](/wiki/Light_pollution "Light pollution") affected country.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

The focal point of the [Bangui Magnetic Anomaly](/wiki/Bangui_Magnetic_Anomaly "Bangui Magnetic Anomaly"), one of the largest [magnetic anomalies](/wiki/Magnetic_anomaly "Magnetic anomaly") on Earth, is located within the country's capital.[[101]](#cite_note-LPI-103)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Climate_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Climate of the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Koppen-Geiger_Map_CAF_present.svg/330px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_CAF_present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_CAF_present.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") map of the Central African Republic

The climate of the Central African Republic is generally [tropical](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics"), with a [wet season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") that lasts from June to September in the northern regions of the country, and from May to October in the south. During the wet season, rainstorms are an almost daily occurrence, and early morning [fog](/wiki/Fog "Fog") is commonplace. Maximum annual precipitation is approximately 1,800 millimetres (71 in) in the upper Ubangi region.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

The northern areas are hot and humid from February to May,[[103]](#cite_note-105) but can be subject to the hot, dry, and dusty [trade wind](/wiki/Trade_wind "Trade wind") known as the [Harmattan](/wiki/Harmattan "Harmattan"). The southern regions have a more equatorial climate, but they are subject to [desertification](/wiki/Desertification "Desertification"), while the extreme northeast regions of the country are a [steppe](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe").[[104]](#cite_note-106)

### Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

Main article: [Wildlife of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Wildlife_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Wildlife of the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Dzanga.jpg/250px-Dzanga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dzanga.jpg)

[Dzanga-Sangha Reserve](/wiki/Dzanga-Sangha_Reserve "Dzanga-Sangha Reserve")

In the southwest, the Dzanga-Sangha National Park is located in a rain forest area. The country is noted for its population of [forest elephants](/wiki/Forest_elephant "Forest elephant") and [western lowland gorillas](/wiki/Western_lowland_gorilla "Western lowland gorilla"). In the north, the [Manovo-Gounda St Floris National Park](/wiki/Manovo-Gounda_St_Floris_National_Park "Manovo-Gounda St Floris National Park") is well-populated with wildlife, including [leopards](/wiki/African_leopard "African leopard"), [lions](/wiki/Central_African_lion "Central African lion"), [cheetahs](/wiki/Sudan_cheetah "Sudan cheetah") and [rhinos](/wiki/Rhinoceros "Rhinoceros"), and the [Bamingui-Bangoran National Park](/wiki/Bamingui-Bangoran_National_Park "Bamingui-Bangoran National Park") is located in the northeast of the Central African Republic. The parks have been seriously affected by the activities of poachers, particularly those from [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan"), over the past two decades.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

In the Central African Republic [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") is around 36% of the total land area, equivalent to 22,303,000 hectares (ha) of forest in 2020, down from 23,203,000 hectares (ha) in 1990. In 2020, naturally regenerating forest covered 22,301,000 hectares (ha) and planted forest covered 2,000 hectares (ha). Of the naturally regenerating forest 9% was reported to be [primary forest](/wiki/Primary_forest "Primary forest") (consisting of native tree species with no clearly visible indications of human activity). For the year 2015, 91% of the forest area was reported to be under [public ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") and 9% [private ownership](/wiki/Private_property "Private property").[[106]](#cite_note-108)[[107]](#cite_note-109) In 2021, the rate of [deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation "Deforestation") in the Central African Republic increased by 71%.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Politics of the Central African Republic"), [Elections in the Central African Republic](/wiki/Elections_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Elections in the Central African Republic"), [Central African Republic Council of Ministers](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Council_of_Ministers "Central African Republic Council of Ministers"), and [List of political parties in the Central African Republic](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Central_African_Republic "List of political parties in the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Faustin-Archange_Touad%C3%A9ra%2C_Joe_Biden%2C_Jill_Biden.jpg/250px-Faustin-Archange_Touad%C3%A9ra%2C_Joe_Biden%2C_Jill_Biden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Faustin-Archange_Touad%C3%A9ra,_Joe_Biden,_Jill_Biden.jpg)

Central African Republic President [Faustin-Archange Touadéra](/wiki/Faustin_Touadera "Faustin Touadera") with US President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), and [Jill Biden](/wiki/Jill_Biden "Jill Biden"), 14 December 2022

Politics in the Central African Republic formally take place in a framework of a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"). In this system, the [President](/wiki/Heads_of_state_of_the_Central_African_Republic_(and_Central_African_Empire) "Heads of state of the Central African Republic (and Central African Empire)") is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"), with a [Prime Minister](/wiki/Heads_of_government_of_the_Central_African_Republic_(and_Central_African_Empire) "Heads of government of the Central African Republic (and Central African Empire)") as [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). [Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is exercised by the government. [Legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power") is vested in both the government and parliament.[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17)

Changes in government have occurred in recent years by three methods: violence, negotiations, and elections. A new constitution was approved by voters in a referendum held on 5 December 2004. The government was rated 'Partly Free' from 1991 to 2001 and from 2004 to 2013.[[109]](#cite_note-111) [V-Dem Democracy Indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_Indices "V-Dem Democracy Indices") described Central African Republic as [autocratizing](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding "Democratic backsliding") in 2024.[[110]](#cite_note-j496-112)

The president is elected by popular vote for a six-year term, and the prime minister is appointed by the president. The president also appoints and presides over the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Central_African_Republic_Council_of_Ministers "Central African Republic Council of Ministers"), which initiates laws and oversees government operations. However, as of 2018 the official government is not in control of large parts of the country, which are governed by rebel groups.[[111]](#cite_note-113) Acting president since April 2016 is [Faustin-Archange Touadéra](/wiki/Faustin_Archange_Touadera "Faustin Archange Touadera") who followed the interim government under [Catherine Samba-Panza](/wiki/Catherine_Samba-Panza "Catherine Samba-Panza"), interim prime minister [André Nzapayeké](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Nzapayek%C3%A9 "André Nzapayeké").[[13]](#cite_note-profile-15)

The [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_the_Central_African_Republic "National Assembly of the Central African Republic") (*Assemblée Nationale*) has 140 members, elected for a five-year term using the two-round (or [run-off](/wiki/Two-round_system "Two-round system")) system.[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17)

As in many other former French colonies, the Central African Republic's legal system is based on [French law](/wiki/French_law "French law").[[112]](#cite_note-114) The Supreme Court, or *Cour Suprême*, is made up of judges appointed by the president. There is also a Constitutional Court, and its judges are also appointed by the president.[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17)

[Freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") is addressed in the country's [constitution](https://www.constituteproject.org/constitution/Central_African_Republic_2013?lang=en#67), but there have been incidents of government intimidation of the media.[[113]](#cite_note-2009_Human_Rights_Report-115) A report by the [International Research & Exchanges Board](/wiki/International_Research_%26_Exchanges_Board "International Research & Exchanges Board")'s media sustainability index noted that "the country minimally met objectives, with segments of the legal system and government opposed to a free media system".[[113]](#cite_note-2009_Human_Rights_Report-115)

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main articles: [Prefectures of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Prefectures_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Prefectures of the Central African Republic") and [Sub-prefectures of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Sub-prefectures_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Sub-prefectures of the Central African Republic")

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/CAR_map_of_prefectures.svg/500px-CAR_map_of_prefectures.svg.png)

A clickable map of the fourteen prefectures of the Central African Republic.

The Central African Republic is divided into 20 administrative [prefectures](/wiki/Prefecture "Prefecture") (*préfectures*), two of which are economic prefectures (*préfectures économiques*); the prefectures are further divided into 84 sub-prefectures (*sous-préfectures*).[[114]](#cite_note-OU-116)

The [prefectures](/wiki/Prefectures_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Prefectures of the Central African Republic") are [Bamingui-Bangoran](/wiki/Bamingui-Bangoran "Bamingui-Bangoran"), [Bangui](/wiki/Bangui_(Prefecture) "Bangui (Prefecture)"), [Basse-Kotto](/wiki/Basse-Kotto "Basse-Kotto"), [Haute-Kotto](/wiki/Haute-Kotto "Haute-Kotto"), [Haut-Mbomou](/wiki/Haut-Mbomou "Haut-Mbomou"), [Kémo](/wiki/K%C3%A9mo "Kémo"), [Lobaye](/wiki/Lobaye "Lobaye"), [Lim-Pendé](/wiki/Lim-Pend%C3%A9 "Lim-Pendé"), [Mambéré](/wiki/Mamb%C3%A9r%C3%A9 "Mambéré"), [Mambéré-Kadéï](/wiki/Mamb%C3%A9r%C3%A9-Kad%C3%A9%C3%AF "Mambéré-Kadéï"), [Mbomou](/wiki/Mbomou "Mbomou"), [Nana-Mambéré](/wiki/Nana-Mamb%C3%A9r%C3%A9 "Nana-Mambéré"), [Ombella-M'Poko](/wiki/Ombella-M%27Poko "Ombella-M'Poko"), [Ouaka](/wiki/Ouaka "Ouaka"), [Ouham](/wiki/Ouham "Ouham"), [Ouham-Fafa](/wiki/Ouham-Fafa "Ouham-Fafa"), [Ouham-Pendé](/wiki/Ouham-Pend%C3%A9 "Ouham-Pendé"), and [Vakaga](/wiki/Vakaga "Vakaga"). The economic prefectures are [Nana-Grébizi](/wiki/Nana-Gr%C3%A9bizi "Nana-Grébizi") and [Sangha-Mbaéré](/wiki/Sangha-Mba%C3%A9r%C3%A9 "Sangha-Mbaéré").[[114]](#cite_note-OU-116)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Foreign relations of the Central African Republic")

See also: [Central African Armed Forces](/wiki/Central_African_Armed_Forces "Central African Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Vladimir_Putin_and_Faustin_Touadera%2C_23_may_2018_%281%29.jpg/250px-Vladimir_Putin_and_Faustin_Touadera%2C_23_may_2018_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vladimir_Putin_and_Faustin_Touadera,_23_may_2018_(1).jpg)

President [Faustin-Archange Touadéra](/wiki/Faustin_Touadera "Faustin Touadera") with Russian President [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin"), 23 May 2018

The Central African Republic is heavily dependent on foreign [aid](/wiki/Aid "Aid"), and numerous [NGOs](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") provide services that the government does not provide.[[115]](#cite_note-117) In 2019, over US$100 million in foreign aid was spent in the country, mostly on humanitarian assistance.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

In 2006, due to ongoing violence, over 50,000 people in the country's northwest were at risk of starvation,[[117]](#cite_note-119) but this was averted due to assistance from the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations").[[118]](#cite_note-UNreport-120) On 8 January 2008, the UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-Moon declared that the Central African Republic was eligible to receive assistance from the Peacebuilding Fund.[[119]](#cite_note-121) Three priority areas were identified: first, the reform of the security sector; second, the promotion of good governance and the rule of law; and third, the revitalization of communities affected by conflicts. On 12 June 2008, the Central African Republic requested assistance from the [UN Peacebuilding Commission](/wiki/UN_Peacebuilding_Commission "UN Peacebuilding Commission"),[[120]](#cite_note-122) which was set up in 2005 to help countries emerging from conflict avoid devolving back into war or chaos.[[121]](#cite_note-123)

In response to concerns of a potential genocide, a peacekeeping force – the International Support Mission to the Central African Republic (MISCA) – was authorized in December 2013. This African Union force of 6,000 personnel was accompanied by the French Operation Sangaris.[[122]](#cite_note-odi.org-124)

In 2017, the Central African Republic signed the UN [Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[123]](#cite_note-125)

In August 2025, Russia demanded that the Central African Republic pay its [Africa Corps](/wiki/Africa_Corps_(Russia) "Africa Corps (Russia)") for armed protection rather than use the private mercenaries [Wagner Group](/wiki/Wagner_Group "Wagner Group").[[124]](#cite_note-126)

### Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Human rights")]

Main article: [Human rights in the Central African Republic](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Human rights in the Central African Republic")

Further information: [LGBTQ rights in the Central African Republic](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_Central_African_Republic "LGBTQ rights in the Central African Republic") and [Polygamy in the Central African Republic](/wiki/Polygamy_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Polygamy in the Central African Republic")

The 2009 Human Rights Report by the [United States Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") noted that human rights in the Central African Republic were poor and expressed concerns over numerous government abuses.[[113]](#cite_note-2009_Human_Rights_Report-115) The [U.S. State Department](/wiki/United_States_State_Department "United States State Department") alleged that major human rights abuses such as [extrajudicial executions](/wiki/Extrajudicial_punishment "Extrajudicial punishment") by security forces, [torture](/wiki/Torture "Torture"), beatings, and [rape](/wiki/Rape "Rape") of suspects and prisoners occurred with impunity. It also alleged harsh and life-threatening conditions in prison and detention centers, [arbitrary arrest](/wiki/Arbitrary_arrest_and_detention "Arbitrary arrest and detention"), prolonged [pretrial detention](/wiki/Remand_(detention) "Remand (detention)") and denial of a [fair trial](/wiki/Right_to_a_fair_trial "Right to a fair trial"), restrictions on freedom of movement, official [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption"), and restrictions on [workers' rights](/wiki/Labor_rights "Labor rights").[[113]](#cite_note-2009_Human_Rights_Report-115)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Living_on_the_rainforest.jpg/250px-Living_on_the_rainforest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Living_on_the_rainforest.jpg)

[Aka](/wiki/Aka_people "Aka people") hunter-gatherers living in the [Dzanga-Sangha Special Reserve](/wiki/Dzanga-Sangha_Special_Reserve "Dzanga-Sangha Special Reserve")

The State Department report also cites widespread [mob violence](/wiki/Riot "Riot"), the prevalence of [female genital mutilation](/wiki/Female_genital_mutilation "Female genital mutilation"), discrimination against women and [pygmies](/wiki/Pygmy_peoples "Pygmy peoples"), [human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking"), [forced labor](/wiki/Unfree_labour "Unfree labour"), and [child labor](/wiki/Child_labor "Child labor").[[125]](#cite_note-127) Freedom of movement is limited in the northern part of the country "because of actions by state security forces, armed bandits, and other non-state armed entities", and due to fighting between government and anti-government forces, many people have been [internally displaced](/wiki/Internally_displaced_person "Internally displaced person").[[126]](#cite_note-state.gov-128)

Violence against children and women in relation to accusations of [witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft") has also been cited as a serious problem in the country.[[127]](#cite_note-unicef.org-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130)[[129]](#cite_note-131) Witchcraft is a criminal offense under the penal code.[[127]](#cite_note-unicef.org-129)

Approximately 68% of girls are married before they turn 18,[[130]](#cite_note-132) and the United Nations's [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") ranked the country 188th out of 188 countries surveyed.[[131]](#cite_note-133) The [Bureau of International Labor Affairs](/wiki/Bureau_of_International_Labor_Affairs "Bureau of International Labor Affairs") has also mentioned it in its last edition of the *[List of Goods Produced by Child Labor or Forced Labor](/wiki/List_of_Goods_Produced_by_Child_Labor_or_Forced_Labor "List of Goods Produced by Child Labor or Forced Labor")*.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Economy of the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Central_African_Republic_GDP_Per_Capita_1960-2022.png/250px-Central_African_Republic_GDP_Per_Capita_1960-2022.png)](/wiki/File:Central_African_Republic_GDP_Per_Capita_1960-2022.png)

GDP per capita (2022 USD)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Bangui_Shopping_District.jpg/250px-Bangui_Shopping_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangui_Shopping_District.jpg)

[Bangui](/wiki/Bangui "Bangui") shopping district

The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") of the Republic is often listed as being approximately $400 a year, [one of the lowest in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GNI (PPP) per capita"), but this figure is based mostly on reported sales of exports and largely ignores the unregistered sale of foods, locally produced [alcoholic beverages](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage"), [diamonds](/wiki/Diamond "Diamond"), [ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory"), [bushmeat](/wiki/Bushmeat "Bushmeat"), and [traditional medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine").[[132]](#cite_note-:2-134)

The currency of the Central African Republic is the [CFA franc](/wiki/Central_African_CFA_franc "Central African CFA franc"), which is accepted across the former countries of [French West Africa](/wiki/French_West_Africa "French West Africa") and trades at a fixed rate to the euro. Diamonds constitute the country's most important export, accounting for 40–55% of export revenues, but it is estimated that between 30% and 50% of those produced each year leave the country clandestinely.[[132]](#cite_note-:2-134) On 27 April 2022,[[133]](#cite_note-135) [Bitcoin](/wiki/Bitcoin "Bitcoin") (BTC) was adopted as an additional legal tender. Lawmakers unanimously adopted a bill that made Bitcoin legal tender alongside the CFA franc and legalized the use of cryptocurrencies. President [Faustin-Archange Touadéra](/wiki/Faustin-Archange_Touad%C3%A9ra "Faustin-Archange Touadéra") signed the measure into law, said his chief of staff Obed Namsio. After an [extraordinary meeting](/wiki/Extraordinary_general_meeting "Extraordinary general meeting") on 6 May 2022, [COBAC](/wiki/COBAC "COBAC") published Decision D-071-2022[[134]](#cite_note-136) in which it banned the use of crypto currency. It subsequently repealed its status as legal tender.[[135]](#cite_note-137)

Agriculture is dominated by the cultivation and sale of food crops such as [cassava](/wiki/Cassava "Cassava"), [peanuts](/wiki/Peanut "Peanut"), [maize](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [sorghum](/wiki/Sorghum "Sorghum"), [millet](/wiki/Millet "Millet"), [sesame](/wiki/Sesame "Sesame"), and [plantain](/wiki/Plantain_(cooking) "Plantain (cooking)"). The annual [growth rate](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") of real GDP is slightly above 3%. The importance of food crops over exported [cash crops](/wiki/Cash_crops "Cash crops") is indicated by the fact that the total production of cassava, the staple food of most Central Africans, ranges between 200,000 and 300,000 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonnes "Tonnes") a year, while the production of [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton"), the principal exported cash crop, ranges from 25,000 to 45,000 tonnes a year. Food crops are not exported in large quantities, but still constitute the principal cash crops of the country because Central Africans derive far more income from the periodic sale of surplus food crops than from exported cash crops such as cotton or coffee.[[132]](#cite_note-:2-134) Much of the country is self-sufficient in food crops; however, [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock") development is hindered by the presence of the [tsetse fly](/wiki/Tsetse_fly "Tsetse fly").[[136]](#cite_note-138)

The Republic's primary import partner is France (17.1%). Other imports come from the United States (12.3%), India (11.5%), and China (8.2%). Its largest export partner is France (31.2%), followed by [Burundi](/wiki/Burundi "Burundi") (16.2%), China (12.5%), [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon") (9.6%), and Austria (7.8%).[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17)

The Central African Republic is a member of the [Organization for the Harmonization of Business Law in Africa](/wiki/Organization_for_the_Harmonization_of_Business_Law_in_Africa "Organization for the Harmonization of Business Law in Africa") (OHADA). In the 2009 [World Bank Group](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group")'s report *Doing Business*, it was ranked 183rd out of 183 as regards 'ease of doing business', a [composite index](/wiki/Composite_(finance) "Composite (finance)") which takes into account regulations that 'enhance' business activity and those that restrict it.[[137]](#cite_note-139)

### Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

#### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transportation in the Central African Republic](/wiki/Transportation_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Transportation in the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Central_African_Republic_-_Trucks_in_Bangui.jpg/250px-Central_African_Republic_-_Trucks_in_Bangui.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_African_Republic_-_Trucks_in_Bangui.jpg)

Trucks in [Bangui](/wiki/Bangui "Bangui")

Two trans-African automobile routes pass through the Central African Republic: the [Tripoli-Cape Town Highway](/wiki/Tripoli-Cape_Town_Highway "Tripoli-Cape Town Highway") and the [Lagos-Mombasa Highway](/wiki/Lagos-Mombasa_Highway "Lagos-Mombasa Highway"). Bangui is the transport hub of the Central African Republic. As of 1999, eight roads connected the city to other main towns in the country, [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon"), Chad, and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan"); of these, only the toll roads are paved. During the rainy season from July to October, some roads are impassable.[[138]](#cite_note-e200-140)[[139]](#cite_note-141)

River [ferries](/wiki/Ferries "Ferries") sail from the [river port](/wiki/River_port "River port") at Bangui to [Brazzaville](/wiki/Brazzaville "Brazzaville") and [Zongo](/wiki/Zongo,_Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Zongo, Democratic Republic of the Congo"). The river can be navigated most of the year between Bangui and Brazzaville. From Brazzaville, goods are transported by rail to [Pointe-Noire](/wiki/Pointe-Noire "Pointe-Noire"), Congo's Atlantic port.[[140]](#cite_note-Finanace-142) The river port handles the overwhelming majority of the country's international trade and has a cargo handling capacity of 350,000 tons; it has 350 metres (1,150 ft) length of [wharfs](/wiki/Wharfs "Wharfs") and 24,000 square metres (260,000 sq ft) of warehousing space.[[138]](#cite_note-e200-140)

[Bangui M'Poko International Airport](/wiki/Bangui_M%27Poko_International_Airport "Bangui M'Poko International Airport") is Central African Republic's only international airport. As of June 2014 it had regularly scheduled direct flights to [Brazzaville](/wiki/Maya-Maya_Airport "Maya-Maya Airport"), [Casablanca](/wiki/Mohammed_V_International_Airport "Mohammed V International Airport"), [Cotonou](/wiki/Cadjehoun_Airport "Cadjehoun Airport"), [Douala](/wiki/Douala_International_Airport "Douala International Airport"), [Kinshasa](/wiki/N%27djili_Airport "N'djili Airport"), [Lomé](/wiki/Lom%C3%A9-Tokoin_Airport "Lomé-Tokoin Airport"), [Luanda](/wiki/Quatro_de_Fevereiro_Airport "Quatro de Fevereiro Airport"), [Malabo](/wiki/Malabo_International_Airport "Malabo International Airport"), [N'Djamena](/wiki/N%27Djamena_International_Airport "N'Djamena International Airport"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris-Charles_de_Gaulle "Paris-Charles de Gaulle"), [Pointe-Noire](/wiki/Pointe_Noire_Airport "Pointe Noire Airport"), and [Yaoundé](/wiki/Yaound%C3%A9_Nsimalen_International_Airport "Yaoundé Nsimalen International Airport").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Since at least 2002 there have been plans to connect Bangui by [rail](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") to the [Transcameroon Railway](/wiki/Transcameroon_Railway "Transcameroon Railway").[[141]](#cite_note-143)

#### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Energy")]

See also: [List of power stations in the Central African Republic](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_the_Central_African_Republic "List of power stations in the Central African Republic")

The Central African Republic primarily uses [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") as there are few other low cost resources for generating electricity.[[142]](#cite_note-144) Access to electricity is very limited with 15.6% of the total population having electrification, 34.6% in urban areas and 1.5% in rural areas.[[143]](#cite_note-WorldFactbook-145)

#### Communications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Communications")]

Main article: [Communications in the Central African Republic](/wiki/Communications_in_the_Central_African_Republic "Communications in the Central African Republic")

Presently, the Central African Republic has active television services, radio stations, [internet service providers](/wiki/Internet_service_providers "Internet service providers"), and [mobile phone carriers](/wiki/Mobile_network_operator "Mobile network operator"); [Socatel](/wiki/Socatel "Socatel") is the leading provider for both internet and mobile phone access throughout the country. The primary governmental regulating bodies of telecommunications are the Ministère des Postes and Télécommunications et des Nouvelles Technologies. In addition, the Central African Republic receives international support on telecommunication related operations from [ITU Telecommunication Development Sector](/wiki/ITU-D "ITU-D") (ITU-D) within the [International Telecommunication Union](/wiki/International_Telecommunication_Union "International Telecommunication Union") to improve infrastructure.[[144]](#cite_note-146)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Central_African_Republic&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of the Central African Republic](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Central_African_Republic "Demographics of the Central African Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Group_of_Peul_women_in_Paoua.jpg/250px-Group_of_Peul_women_in_Paoua.jpg)](/wiki/File:Group_of_Peul_women_in_Paoua.jpg)

Fula women in [Paoua](/wiki/Paoua "Paoua")

The population of the Central African Republic has almost quadrupled since independence. In 1960, the population was 1,232,000; as of a 2021 UN estimate, it is approximately 5,457,154.[[145]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-147)[[146]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-148)

The [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") estimates that approximately 4% of the population aged between 15 and 49 is [HIV](/wiki/HIV "HIV") positive.[[147]](#cite_note-unaids-149) Only 3% of the country has [antiretroviral](/wiki/Antiretroviral "Antiretroviral") therapy available, compared to 17% coverage in the neighboring countries of [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad") and the [Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Congo "Republic of the Congo").[[148]](#cite_note-150)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

The nation comprises over 80 ethnic groups, each having its own language. The largest ethnic groups are the [Baggara Arabs](/wiki/Baggara "Baggara"), [Baka](/wiki/Baka_people_(Cameroon_and_Gabon) "Baka people (Cameroon and Gabon)"), [Banda](/wiki/Banda_people "Banda people"), [Bayaka](/wiki/Aka_people "Aka people"), [Fula](/wiki/Fula_people "Fula people"), [Gbaya](/wiki/Gbaya_people "Gbaya people"), [Kara](/wiki/Kara_people "Kara people"), [Kresh](/wiki/Kresh_people "Kresh people"), [Mbaka](/wiki/Mbaka_people "Mbaka people"), [Mandja](/wiki/Mandja_people "Mandja people"), [Ngbandi](/wiki/Ngbandi_people "Ngbandi people"), [Sara](/wiki/Sara_people "Sara people"), [Vidiri](/wiki/Vidiri_people "Vidiri people"), [Wodaabe](/wiki/Wodaabe "Wodaabe"), [Yakoma](/wiki/Yakoma_people "Yakoma people"), [Yulu](/wiki/Yulu_people "Yulu people"), and [Zande](/wiki/Zande_people "Zande people"), with [others](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Central_African_Republic#Ethnic_groups "Demographics of the Central African Republic") including [Europeans](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") of mostly [French descent](/wiki/French_people "French people").[[15]](#cite_note-CIA-17) The most common ethnic groups are [Gbaya](/wiki/Gbaya_people "Gbaya people") (Baya) (28.8%) and [Banda](/wiki/Banda_people "Banda people") (22.9%), comprising together slightly over half of the country's population in 2003.[[149]](#cite_note-151)
